result_summary: Comprehensive operational readiness audit of AURA v4 daemon and neocortex. 12 findings identified across startup, shutdown, crash recovery, resource management, and Android lifecycle. 2 CRITICAL issues (JNI shutdown disconnect), 3 HIGH, 3 MEDIUM, 2 LOW, 2 INFO. Architecture is fundamentally sound — bounded channels, atomic checkpoints, graceful degradation — but the JNI↔daemon shutdown path is broken and must be fixed before production.

audit_metadata.scope: Startup/shutdown sequences, crash recovery, resource management, Android lifecycle

findings.title: JNI nativeShutdown is a no-op — cannot trigger daemon shutdown from Android
findings.description: The JNI nativeShutdown() function (lib.rs:159-169) only logs 'JNI shutdown requested' and returns. It does NOT set the daemon's cancel_flag, call graceful_shutdown(), or signal the tokio runtime in any way. The raw pointer to DaemonState cannot be safely dereferenced because run() consumed ownership. When Android's foreground service calls nativeShutdown() from onDestroy(), absolutely nothing happens on the Rust side — the daemon continues running as an orphan until the process is killed.
findings.recommendation: Extract cancel_flag (Arc<AtomicBool>) during nativeInit() and store it in a static AtomicPtr or OnceCell accessible to nativeShutdown(). When nativeShutdown() is called, load the cancel_flag and store(true, Release). The main loop already checks this flag at line 1581 of main_loop.rs — it will exit cleanly. This is a 15-line fix that fits perfectly into the existing architecture.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: On Android, the daemon NEVER shuts down gracefully. Every service stop, app update, or system-initiated kill results in an unclean exit — no final checkpoint, no channel drain, no database close. Data loss on every shutdown.

findings.title: cancel_flag created in DaemonState but never exposed to JNI layer
findings.description: The cancel_flag (Arc<AtomicBool>) is created during startup() Phase 8 (startup.rs) and stored inside DaemonState. It is properly checked at the top of the main loop (main_loop.rs:1581) and cloned into bridge tasks. However, it is never returned or stored anywhere accessible from the JNI boundary. This is the root cause of ORA-001 — the shutdown mechanism EXISTS and WORKS, but there is no way to activate it from outside the tokio runtime.
findings.recommendation: During Phase 8 of startup(), clone the cancel_flag and store it in a module-level static (e.g., static CANCEL_FLAG: OnceLock<Arc<AtomicBool>>). In nativeShutdown(), load from the static and set it. Alternatively, return the cancel_flag from startup() alongside DaemonState and store it in a JNI-accessible location during nativeInit().
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Same as ORA-001 — graceful shutdown is completely unreachable from Android. These two findings form a single logical defect.

findings.title: JNI nativeInit ignores configJson parameter — Kotlin-side config is discarded
findings.description: AuraDaemonBridge.kt calls nativeInit(dataDir, cacheDir, buildConfigJson()) passing a JSON config string that includes device model, Android API level, and build type. The Rust JNI init() function (lib.rs:111) receives this parameter but ignores it entirely, using AuraConfig::default() instead. Any Android-specific configuration (data paths, device capabilities, API-level-dependent behavior) is lost.
findings.recommendation: Deserialize the configJson parameter in JNI init() and merge it into AuraConfig. At minimum, extract data_dir and cache_dir from the JNI arguments (they are already passed). The existing AuraConfig likely has fields for these — wire them through.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Daemon uses hardcoded default paths instead of Android-provided paths. On a real device this means files written to wrong locations, potential permission errors, or silent data loss when Android cleans directories the daemon didn't expect.

findings.title: tokio::runtime::Builder .expect() in JNI run() — panic crashes Android process
findings.description: In lib.rs:146, the tokio runtime is built with .enable_all().build().expect('runtime'). If the runtime fails to build (e.g., exhausted file descriptors, thread spawn failure), this panics. In a JNI context, a Rust panic unwinds across the FFI boundary, which is undefined behavior in practice and will crash the entire Android app process.
findings.recommendation: Replace .expect() with proper error handling: match on the Result, and on failure, log the error and call the JNI error callback (or set a JNI exception) to gracefully report the failure to the Kotlin layer. Consider using catch_unwind as a safety net around the entire run() body, consistent with how startup.rs handles subsystem init.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Under resource pressure (low FDs, memory pressure), the daemon can panic and crash the entire Android app. The user sees an app crash with no recovery — the foreground service dies, BootReceiver won't help until next reboot.

findings.title: Neocortex shutdown relies on stdin-close — fragile signal mechanism
findings.description: The neocortex process (aura-neocortex/src/main.rs:392-410) uses a background thread that blocks on stdin.read_line(). When stdin closes (parent dies), it fires the ctrlc handler to initiate shutdown. This is fragile: (1) stdin may be redirected or closed for other reasons, (2) if the parent daemon crashes without closing the pipe, the neocortex hangs forever, (3) on some Android configurations stdin behavior is unpredictable in background processes.
findings.recommendation: Add a secondary keepalive mechanism: the daemon sends periodic heartbeat messages over the IPC TCP connection. If the neocortex doesn't receive a heartbeat within 2x the interval, it self-terminates. This is additive — keep the stdin-close mechanism as a backup. Alternatively, use the TCP connection close as the primary signal (when the daemon's TCP client disconnects, the neocortex server detects it and shuts down).
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Orphaned neocortex processes accumulate after daemon crashes, consuming memory (LLM model stays loaded). On a memory-constrained Android device, this leads to OOM kills of other apps or the system itself.

findings.title: AuraMemory::new_in_memory().expect() can panic during main loop setup
findings.description: In main_loop.rs:1234-1235, during the pre-select setup phase, the real AuraMemory is moved out of DaemonState via std::mem::replace, and a placeholder in-memory instance is created with .expect(). If this allocation fails (unlikely but possible under memory pressure), the daemon panics. This is after startup completed successfully, so there's no catch_unwind safety net at this point.
findings.recommendation: Replace .expect() with match and graceful error handling. On failure, skip the memory swap and continue with a degraded mode (or log and proceed with the original memory instance). Alternatively, wrap the entire run() function body in catch_unwind at the top level.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Low probability but high impact — under extreme memory pressure, the daemon panics after startup, bypassing all shutdown logic. The checkpoint from the successful startup is still valid, so crash recovery works, but the user experience is an unexpected crash.

findings.title: ThermalCritical handler has no mechanism to pause LLM inference
findings.description: In main_loop.rs handle_health_event (line 4946), ThermalCritical events are received but the handler only has a TODO comment. There is no API on NeocortexClient to pause or throttle inference. When the device is thermally throttling, the LLM continues running at full speed, exacerbating thermal issues and potentially triggering Android thermal shutdown.
findings.recommendation: Add a Pause/Resume message type to the DaemonToNeocortex IPC protocol. On ThermalCritical, send Pause. On thermal recovery (next heartbeat with acceptable temps), send Resume. The neocortex side should queue but not process inference requests while paused. As an interim fix, set safe_mode.active = true on thermal critical (reusing existing infrastructure) to block new inference requests at the daemon level.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Sustained thermal events lead to Android thermal shutdown or hardware throttling that degrades the entire device. Battery drain accelerates. User's phone gets uncomfortably hot.

findings.title: Neocortex server uses blocking poll loop with 100ms sleep — not async
findings.description: The neocortex TCP server (main.rs) uses TcpListener::set_nonblocking(true) with a manual poll loop that sleeps 100ms between accept attempts. This wastes CPU cycles on polling and adds up to 100ms latency on every new connection. For a single-client IPC scenario this is functional but inefficient, especially on battery-constrained Android.
findings.recommendation: This is working code and should NOT be rewritten speculatively. However, if the neocortex is ever refactored, migrating to tokio TcpListener would eliminate the poll loop overhead. For now, consider reducing the sleep to 10-20ms to reduce connection latency, or use a longer sleep (500ms) in low-power mode to reduce CPU wakeups.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Minor battery drain from unnecessary CPU wakeups every 100ms. 50-100ms added latency on IPC connection establishment. Not a correctness issue.

findings.title: JNI shutdown completion callback is a stub — Kotlin never learns shutdown finished
findings.description: shutdown.rs:221-230 defines jni_notify_shutdown_complete() which only logs 'JNI shutdown complete callback (stub)'. The Kotlin AuraForegroundService.onDestroy() calls nativeShutdown() and then Thread.join(5000) — it waits for the daemon thread to exit but has no callback mechanism to know if shutdown was clean or timed out.
findings.recommendation: After fixing ORA-001/002 (so shutdown actually works), implement the JNI callback: store the JNI GlobalRef to the AuraDaemonBridge instance during nativeInit(), and in jni_notify_shutdown_complete(), call a Kotlin method (e.g., onDaemonShutdownComplete(boolean clean)). This lets Kotlin decide whether to show a notification, retry, or report telemetry.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Kotlin layer has no visibility into whether shutdown was clean. The 5s thread join timeout is the only signal — if it times out, Kotlin doesn't know if data was saved. Low risk because Android will kill the process anyway, but it prevents implementing smart recovery strategies.

findings.title: Checkpoint goals vector is unbounded — can exceed 64KB checkpoint limit
findings.description: state.checkpoint.goals is a Vec<Goal> that is pushed to in multiple places (handle_ipc_inbound PlanReady, BDI deliberation cron). There is a MAX_ACTIVE_GOALS constant that limits the BDI system, but completed/failed goals are not pruned from the checkpoint vector. Over a long-running session, accumulated goals could cause the checkpoint to exceed the 64KB bincode limit, at which point save_checkpoint() silently fails (or panics, depending on the serialization path).
findings.recommendation: Add a goal pruning step to the checkpoint save path: before serialization, remove goals in terminal states (Completed, Failed, Cancelled) older than a configurable retention window (e.g., 24h). Alternatively, cap the vector length and evict oldest terminal goals. Also add a size guard in save_checkpoint() that logs a warning if serialized size exceeds 48KB (75% of limit).
findings.risk_if_unfixed: After extended use (weeks/months), checkpoint saves silently fail. The daemon continues running but loses crash recovery capability — next restart falls back to defaults, losing personality evolution, trust scores, and active goals.

findings.title: bridge/retry.rs does not exist — specified audit target is missing
findings.description: The bridge module contains telegram_bridge.rs, system_api.rs, router.rs, voice_bridge.rs, and mod.rs. No retry.rs file exists. If retry logic was planned for bridge reconnection (Telegram, voice, IPC), it either hasn't been implemented yet or is inlined in individual bridge files.
findings.recommendation: Verify whether bridge reconnection retry logic exists inline in telegram_bridge.rs and voice_bridge.rs. If not, consider implementing exponential backoff retry for bridge connections, especially Telegram (network can be flaky on mobile). This is not urgent — bridges already handle disconnection gracefully by closing their channel.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: If bridge retry logic is missing, a transient network failure permanently disconnects Telegram until daemon restart. Not a crash risk but a usability issue.

findings.title: Database tables created lazily via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS in handle_db_write
findings.description: Rather than creating all tables during startup Phase 3 (Database Open), tables are created on first write via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS in handle_db_write (main_loop.rs:4977+). This is functionally correct but means the schema isn't guaranteed to exist until the first write to each table type. If a read query runs before the first write, it will fail with 'no such table'.
findings.recommendation: Move CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS statements to startup Phase 3 (after database open, before state restore). This is a minor improvement — the current approach works because reads are protected by Option checks, but explicit schema initialization is more predictable and enables schema migration during startup.
findings.risk_if_unfixed: Potential for confusing 'no such table' errors in edge cases where a subsystem queries a table before any writes. Low probability because the daemon's write-heavy nature means tables are typically created early in the session.

positive_findings.title: Atomic checkpoint writes with crash-safe rename pattern
positive_findings.description: Checkpoint saves write to .bin.tmp, sync_all(), then rename() — textbook crash-safe atomic write pattern. Corruption from power loss or process kill during write is impossible.

positive_findings.title: Schema migration framework with sequential version upgrades
positive_findings.description: CHECKPOINT_VERSION = 3 with v1→v2→v3 migration chain. Adding new checkpoint fields requires only a new migration function — backwards-compatible evolution.

positive_findings.title: Bounded everything — channels, vectors, queues, message sizes
positive_findings.description: All 9 mpsc channels have explicit capacity bounds. BoundedVec for health monitoring. pending_system2_sources capped at 64. MAX_ACTIVE_GOALS limit. Message truncation at 4096 chars. MAX_IPC_PAYLOAD_BYTES. Telemetry capped at 16KB. RawSql params limited to 32. This is excellent defense-in-depth against resource exhaustion.

positive_findings.title: Graceful degradation — non-critical subsystems fail to None
positive_findings.description: Startup separates critical (Memory, Identity, Executor, Planner) from non-critical subsystems. Non-critical subsystems use catch_unwind and degrade to None on failure. The daemon starts and runs with reduced capability rather than crashing.

positive_findings.title: Safe mode blocks learning and proactive actions after anomalies
positive_findings.description: When safe_mode.active is set (memory pressure, crash recovery), the daemon blocks learning, outcome dispatch, and proactive actions. This prevents cascading failures from corrupted state.

positive_findings.title: Multi-layer security gates at 6+ enforcement points
positive_findings.description: Consent → Sandbox → BoundaryReasoner → PolicyGate → Emergency Stop applied consistently at UserCommand TaskRequest, PlanReady, ComposedScript, proactive actions, and more. Defense-in-depth against unauthorized actions.

positive_findings.title: WakeLock management with 10-min timeout and 9-min renewal
positive_findings.description: AuraDaemonBridge uses a Handler to renew the partial WakeLock every 9 minutes before the 10-minute timeout expires. Proper synchronized access. This prevents the daemon from being suspended during inference while avoiding indefinite WakeLock holds.

positive_findings.title: Main loop dual-exit mechanism — cancel_flag + channel exhaustion
positive_findings.description: The main loop exits when either cancel_flag is set OR all channels close (open_channels == 0). This means the loop exits cleanly whether shutdown is signaled explicitly or all producers complete naturally.

positive_findings.title: Power-aware cron with multi-condition guards
positive_findings.description: Dreaming requires charging + battery >= 30% + thermal < 45C + user idle >= 30min. Non-critical cron jobs skip in Critical/Emergency power states. Deep consolidation skips if battery < 15%. This is thoughtful battery-conscious design for a mobile platform.

phases.name: JNI Load
phases.description: Validates JNI environment on Android, stub on host. Extracts data_dir, cache_dir from JNI strings.

phases.name: Runtime Init
phases.description: Initializes tracing subscriber for structured logging.

phases.name: Database Open
phases.description: Opens SQLite with WAL mode, 4MB mmap, 4096 page_size. Creates connection pool.
phases.failure_mode: StartupError — daemon cannot start without database

phases.name: State Restore
phases.description: Loads DaemonCheckpoint from state.bin (bincode). Falls back to defaults on corruption or missing file. Applies schema migrations if needed.

phases.name: Subsystem Init
phases.description: Initializes all subsystems. Critical (Memory, Identity, Executor, Planner) must succeed. Non-critical wrapped in catch_unwind, degrade to None.
phases.failure_mode: Critical subsystem failure → StartupError. Non-critical → degraded mode (Option::None).

phases.name: IPC Bind
phases.description: Binds abstract socket on Android for neocortex IPC. Deferred on host.
phases.failure_mode: StartupError — no IPC means no LLM inference capability

phases.name: Cron Schedule
phases.description: Rebuilds timer heap from checkpoint cron state. Registers 25+ periodic jobs.
phases.failure_mode: Fallback to default schedule on corruption

phases.name: Ready
phases.description: Creates DaemonChannels (9 bounded mpsc), cancel_flag (Arc<AtomicBool>). Returns DaemonState ready for main loop.
phases.failure_mode: Allocation failure would panic (no catch_unwind here)

startup_sequence_map.post_startup: Onboarding check: FirstRun → prompt, Interrupted → resume, Completed → skip

shutdown_sequence_map.trigger: cancel_flag.store(true, Release) — BUT currently unreachable from JNI (see ORA-001/002)

steps.name: Signal Cancellation
steps.description: Sets cancel_flag to true with Release ordering. Main loop checks with Acquire ordering at top of each iteration.

steps.name: Drain Channels
steps.description: Drops all tx halves to signal producers. Then try_recv() loops on all 9 rx channels to drain buffered messages. Synchronous — does not process messages, just discards.

steps.name: Flush Final Checkpoint
steps.description: Calls save_checkpoint() to persist daemon state to disk. Uses atomic write (tmp + sync + rename).

steps.name: Close Database
steps.description: Calls conn.close(). If close() returns Err (uncommitted transaction), drops the connection as fallback.

steps.name: Report Completion
steps.description: Calls jni_notify_shutdown_complete() — currently a stub that only logs (see ORA-009).

shutdown_sequence_map.on_timeout: Each step checks Instant::now() >= deadline. If exceeded, remaining steps are skipped and function returns early. Final checkpoint may be lost if timeout occurs before Step 3.
known_gaps: In-flight neocortex inference is not cancelled — no Abort/Cancel message in IPC protocol
Spawned tokio tasks (ResponseRouter, bridges, heartbeat) are not explicitly cancelled — they rely on channel closure to exit
JNI callback is a stub — Kotlin never learns shutdown status

crash_recovery_assessment.checkpoint_integrity: STRONG — atomic writes, schema migration, corruption fallback to defaults
state_lost_on_crash: Pending System2 source routing queue (VecDeque in memory only)
Working memory entries not yet persisted to SQLite
crash_recovery_assessment.recovery_mechanism: On restart, startup Phase 4 loads last successful checkpoint. Safe mode activates to block learning/proactive actions until stability confirmed. Neocortex starts disconnected and reconnects via health monitor.
crash_recovery_assessment.crash_loop_prevention: Not explicitly implemented. If the daemon crashes repeatedly during startup (e.g., corrupted database), it will restart indefinitely via Android's START_STICKY + BootReceiver. Consider adding a crash counter to the checkpoint and entering minimal-safe-mode after N consecutive crashes.

android_lifecycle_assessment.foreground_service: GOOD — proper FOREGROUND_SERVICE_TYPE_SPECIAL_USE, START_STICKY, notification channel
android_lifecycle_assessment.wakelock: GOOD — 10-min partial WakeLock with 9-min Handler renewal, synchronized access
android_lifecycle_assessment.anr_risk: LOW — nativeInit/nativeRun dispatched to background thread, nativeShutdown on background thread with timeouts
android_lifecycle_assessment.battery_optimization: GOOD — power-aware cron, dreaming guards, initiative budget capping on low battery
android_lifecycle_assessment.accessibility_service: GOOD — proper node recycling, immutable sensor snapshots with atomic swap
known_gaps: No handling of Android doze mode transitions
No handling of battery optimization whitelisting prompts
No crash-loop detection at Android layer (only START_STICKY restarts)

next_steps.action: Fix ORA-001 + ORA-002: Wire cancel_flag from DaemonState to JNI-accessible static
next_steps.impact: Enables graceful shutdown from Android — unlocks the entire shutdown sequence that already exists and works

next_steps.action: Fix ORA-004: Replace .expect() on tokio runtime build with proper error handling
next_steps.impact: Prevents Android process crash under resource pressure

next_steps.action: Fix ORA-003: Wire configJson from JNI to AuraConfig
next_steps.impact: Ensures daemon uses correct Android-provided paths and device config

next_steps.action: Fix ORA-005: Add secondary keepalive/disconnect detection for neocortex
next_steps.effort: ~30 lines across daemon IPC client and neocortex server
next_steps.impact: Prevents orphaned neocortex processes after daemon crash

next_steps.action: Fix ORA-007: Implement ThermalCritical response (interim: activate safe_mode)
next_steps.effort: ~5 lines for interim fix, ~40 lines for proper IPC pause/resume
next_steps.impact: Prevents thermal runaway on Android devices

next_steps.action: Fix ORA-010: Add goal pruning to checkpoint save path
next_steps.impact: Prevents long-term checkpoint bloat and silent save failures
